intro:
Getting the right number of tiles is critical for any tiling project — too few means a mid-project store run that may result in a different batch with slight color variation, while too many means wasted money. Our tile calculator determines exactly how many tiles you need by dividing your room area by each tile's area, then adding a waste factor for cuts and breakage.

The standard waste factor recommendation is 10% for straight-lay patterns, 15% for diagonal or herringbone patterns, and up to 20% for rooms with many obstacles (multiple doorways, irregular shapes). Our default is 10% — adjust it upward for complex patterns or rooms.

Tile is typically sold by the square foot, box, or individual piece. Once you have the tile count, note the square footage per box on packaging and buy the next full box above your requirement — always from the same production lot to ensure color and size consistency.
howItWorks: Enter your room length and width in feet, and tile dimensions in inches. The calculator converts tile size to square feet, divides room area by tile area to get base tile count, then applies your waste factor percentage to give a final tiles-needed figure with buffer.
interpretationGuide: Tiles needed includes your specified waste buffer. Always order this full amount — do not cut the waste factor to save money, as finding matching tiles later from a different dye lot is difficult. Convert to boxes by dividing by tiles-per-box shown on packaging.

faqs.question: What waste factor should I use for different tile patterns?
faqs.answer: Straight lay: 10%. Diagonal lay (45°): 15–20% (more cuts at edges). Herringbone or chevron: 15–20%. Large-format tiles (24×24 or bigger): add 5% extra since mistakes are more costly. Simple rectangular rooms: 10% is fine. Irregular rooms with many obstacles: use 15%.

faqs.question: How do I calculate tiles needed for a bathroom floor?
faqs.answer: Measure the length and width of the floor (including areas under vanities and behind the toilet). Multiply for square footage. Enter tile dimensions in the calculator. For a 5×8 bathroom (40 sq ft) with 12×12 tiles at 10% waste: ~44 tiles.

faqs.question: What is a dye lot and why does it matter?
faqs.answer: Ceramic and porcelain tiles are fired in batches (dye lots). Slight color and size variations exist between different production runs of the same tile. Always verify all boxes have the same lot number, and order enough in one purchase to complete the project plus future repairs.

faqs.question: Should I include the grout joint in tile spacing?
faqs.answer: Yes. Standard grout joints are 1/16" (rectified tiles) to 1/4" (irregular tiles). Larger grout joints slightly reduce the number of tiles needed. For most home tiling projects, the adjustment is minimal and factored into the waste margin.

faqs.question: How many tiles come in a box?
faqs.answer: It depends on tile size. 12×12 tiles: typically 15 tiles per box (~15 sq ft). 24×24 tiles: 4–5 tiles per box (~16 sq ft). Subway tiles (3×6): 30–40 tiles per box (~5 sq ft). Always check the box for exact tile count and square footage.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ceramic and porcelain tile?
faqs.answer: Porcelain is denser and less porous than ceramic (absorbs less than 0.5% water vs. ceramic's 3%+). Porcelain is harder, more durable, and suitable for floors in all climates. Ceramic is easier to cut and less expensive. Both are good for interior floors and walls; use porcelain for outdoor or high-moisture areas.

faqs.question: How do I calculate tiles for a backsplash?
faqs.answer: Measure the backsplash area: length × height (typically 18 inches between counter and cabinets). Subtract the stove hood area if applicable. For a 10-foot kitchen with 1.5-foot backsplash height: 10 × 1.5 = 15 sq ft. Add 15% for cuts and patterns.

faqs.question: What size tile should I use for different spaces?
faqs.answer: Small bathrooms: 12×12 or smaller makes the room feel larger when used correctly. Large open floors: 18×18 or 24×24 tiles reduce grout lines and look more expansive. Showers: 4×4 to 12×12 for walls, small mosaic for shower floors (for grip). Kitchen floors: 12×12 to 18×18 is standard.

faqs.question: What tools do I need to cut tiles?
faqs.answer: Manual tile cutter: good for straight cuts on ceramic. Angle grinder with diamond blade: cuts curves and notches. Wet tile saw (rent or buy): best for porcelain and precision cuts. Nippers: for small irregular pieces. For a medium-sized bathroom, a manual cutter and nippers suffice for most cuts.

faqs.question: How do I start tiling to minimize cuts?
faqs.answer: Start from the center of the room and work outward, ensuring cut tiles at the perimeter are as equal and large as possible. Avoid narrow slivers at highly visible doorways. Dry-lay tiles without adhesive first to plan your layout and starting point before committing.
